summary: DHH enthusiastically reviews Jason Hickel's book 'Less Is More,' which argues that capitalism's need for never-ending growth is destroying the planet. He traces his discovery of Hickel through the Citations Needed podcast and his earlier book 'The Divide,' praising the author's accessible writing style. The review summarizes the book's arc: establishing ecological stakes with visceral examples, providing a revisionist history of capitalism from feudalism through industrialization, and arguing that GDP growth beyond a certain point doesn't improve human wellbeing. DHH calls it imperative reading and urges action before it's too late.
key_points: Capitalism's requirement for perpetual growth is causing ecological destruction observable within a single generation
Life before and outside capitalism wasn't the 'short and brutish' misery commonly depicted — life expectancy actually fell during early industrialization
Societies with far lower GDP per capita than the US deliver longer-living, happier citizens, undermining the growth-equals-progress narrative
The common framing of the global south as 'developing countries' relies on accounting shenanigans to maintain the illusion that world poverty is declining
Hickel's writing is praised as plain, accessible, and flowing compared to denser works like Piketty's Capital
money_quotes: Capitalism's need for never-ending growth is killing the planet.
If you're even just a forty-something like myself, you can actually remember when the earth was different. That's... heavy.
Life prior to capitalism wasn't the 'short and brutish' misery it's commonly depicted as, in fact much of the time it is and was the opposite, particularly at the start of the industrial revolution.
The never-ending pursuits of capitalism are not making us live healthier, happier lives once we've reached a certain point of maturity.
There are societies around the world that make due with a small slice of the GDP-per-capita that, say, the US musters, yet deliver far longer living, happier citizens.
The constant search for growth is killing the planet, and we're watching it happen in real time.
It debunked myths I'd left unquestioned since attending business school.
This is imperative reading for anyone living, working, or succeeding under capitalism as we know it today.
key_insight: The relentless pursuit of economic growth has passed the point of improving human lives and is now actively destroying the planet, making degrowth thinking urgent and necessary.
